copy: What's one indicator you swore by when you started trading that you'd never use now?
SPY opens red. Do you buy the dip or wait for confirmation? Be honest.
Worst trade you've taken this month. No judgement. We've all got one.
Hot take: most traders would make more money if they traded LESS. Agree or disagree?
How long did it take you to stop revenge trading? Or are you still working on it?
VIX is at 14. Does that make you comfortable or nervous? Genuinely curious.
Name a ticker you're watching right now but haven't pulled the trigger on yet.
Do you check futures before bed? Or is that just a recipe for not sleeping?
Your portfolio is flat for 3 months. Do you change your strategy or trust the process?
Unpopular opinion: the best edge in markets isn't a strategy. It's patience.
How many open positions are you holding right now? Over/under 5.
What's the one mistake you keep making even though you know better?
Friday afternoon. Market's about to close. Are you holding over the weekend or going flat?
If you could only trade ONE ETF for the rest of your life, what are you picking?
Real question: do you actually have a trading journal or do you just wing it?
Bonds are selling off and nobody in your feed is talking about it. That's usually when you should pay attention.
The hardest part of trading isn't finding entries. It's sitting in a winner without touching it.
Be honest. How much of your P&L this year came from 2 or 3 trades?
What's a market lesson you had to learn the expensive way?
Do you trade the open or wait 30 minutes? I'm curious what the split is.
Every time VIX gets this low, people forget what a real selloff feels like. Then it shows up.
If you're not tracking what the market did on similar days in the past, you're guessing. Even if it's an educated guess.
Biggest lie in trading: "I'll add more when it pulls back." You won't. You'll chase it higher.
Market goes up 6 days straight. Are you buying the momentum or expecting a pullback? No wrong answers.
What's the first thing you check every morning before the market opens?
Gold at all-time highs while stocks are also at highs. When does that usually end well? (It doesn't.)
Chart patterns or macro context? Pick one. I already know my answer.
The people who made the most money this year aren't the ones who traded the most. They're the ones who sized up when it mattered.
Everyone's a genius in a bull market. The real test is what you do when the trend breaks.
Quick poll: do you think we finish the year higher or lower from here?
The market just went 8 sessions without a 1% move. That kind of calm never lasts.
Name a sector you're bullish on that nobody else is talking about.
Crypto traders and stock traders looking at the same macro data and reaching completely different conclusions. Tale as old as time.
How many screens do you trade with? I genuinely want to know if more screens = better decisions or just more distractions.
Overtrading has cost me more money than any bad thesis ever has. Learning when to sit out is the real edge.
Oil moved and nobody's talking about it. $USO is repricing inflation risk while everyone watches $SPY. Model caught this days ago.
Money is quietly moving into utilities while indexes look fine. When big money hides before anything breaks, pay attention.
Dollar weakening. Usually good for growth. Model flipped to this read two days ago. Most of Twitter still hasn't caught on.
VIX structure changed. Buyers stopped pressing for more upside. Everyone's calling breakout. That disconnect usually ends badly.
$SPY chopping sideways and everyone's bored. Underneath, money is rotating. The index looks dead. The internals say otherwise.
$QQQ dipped and got bought right back. Bears pressed for follow-through and got nothing. That's a failed breakdown, not weakness.
$IWM diverging from $SPY. Small caps getting accumulated while the index goes nowhere. Boring tapes are where moves start.
Gold accelerating while stocks are still going up. That combo doesn't usually last. Someone big is buying protection.
$QQQ is being carried by 3 names. That's not a healthy trend, that's a house of cards. When those names rotate, so does the index.
Bonds getting bid while stocks are green. Big money is buying insurance quietly. Surface says bullish. Bond market says careful.
Credit spreads tightening while VIX creeps higher. Mixed signal. Last 3 times this happened, the move came within a week.
Semis leading again. When $SOXX outperforms $SPY for 5+ sessions, the trend usually has legs. Model confirmed this morning.
Everyone watching the Fed. The bond market already told you what it thinks. $TLT has been moving for a week.
Volatility this low makes people complacent. The data says these setups have a shelf life. The model is watching the clock.
Model said risk-on yesterday. $SPY gapped up and never looked back. The data had the answer before the bell rang.
$NVDA led. $QQQ followed. Model said growth holds as long as credit stays open. It did. Dip buyers won.
Model tagged a squeeze setup before $SPY broke higher. Half of Twitter was shorting resistance. The data said squeeze. It squeezed.
First red candle and everyone panicked. Model said this pattern recovers. $QQQ was green by lunch. Best trade: don't sell the shakeout.
Credit held. Vol stayed low. $SPY kept grinding. Model said continuation. That's exactly what happened. Bears have been wrong for weeks.
Model flagged semis before the move hit $QQQ. By the time Twitter saw it, the easy money was made.
Called risk-off on Monday. $SPY dropped 1.2% by Wednesday. Not bragging, just showing the data works.
Wrong call yesterday. Model said risk-on, market sold off. Happens. Track record is still 68% over 30 days. One miss doesn't break that.
RSI overbought doesn't mean sell. $SPY has run overbought for weeks in strong trends. You need historical context, not just an indicator.
Indicators show what price already did. Pattern matching shows what price usually does next. One reacts. One anticipates.
The market doesn't care about your favourite indicator. It rewards knowing when conditions are dangerous vs. normal.
"RSI is overbought so I'm shorting." How many times has that cost you? Overbought stays overbought for weeks in a strong market.
9 ETFs. 10 years of data. One number each morning that tells you if the market is risk-on, risk-off, or neutral. That's the model.
Most people look at $SPY and call the market. We check bonds, credit, gold, oil, volatility, and the dollar. Different picture.
Free email every morning before the bell. Risk-on, risk-off, or neutral. 9 ETFs. 10 years of data. 90-second read.
One number. Every morning. Before you trade. Based on macro data, not vibes. Free daily briefing, no account needed.
Most newsletters give you someone's opinion. This one gives you a number backed by 10 years of pattern matching. Free. Daily.
Do you actually know if the market is risk-on or risk-off right now? Not what Twitter thinks. What the data shows. That's what I send every morning.
$BTC consolidating above support while $ETH underperforms. The model reads this as accumulation. Similar setups have resolved higher.
Crypto vol compressing while equities chop. Last 3 times this happened, BTC moved 8%+ within 10 days. Direction TBD.
$BTC broke out while stocks went nowhere. Cross-asset divergence like this usually means one of them is wrong. Model says watch DXY.
Everyone's watching BTC price. Nobody's watching BTC vs gold. That ratio is telling a different story right now.
Free daily crypto bias score. Same approach as our macro model but tuned for BTC and ETH. Data, not hopium.
